hand.title: Daily Hand

events.text: Your cards — Q, 6. You raised aggressively before the flop, putting pressure on your opponent, who calmly called to see the next cards.
Flop — Queen, 9, 8. You bet to test your opponent's resolve, and they called, still in the hand with a high card.
Turn — 5. You checked, giving your opponent the opportunity to act, and they bet — bigger than expected — having just improved to one pair.
River — 9. Your opponent fired again for value, confident in their two pair hand, looking to maximize their winnings.

wrongReasons: 5 6 has similar strength, but look at when the aggression shifted — the turn card connects with the correct hand in a way it doesn't connect with 5 6.
9 Q makes a full house here — a hand that strong would have raised earlier and pushed harder for maximum value. The measured sizing in this hand points to something weaker.
2 10 doesn't benefit from the turn card the way the correct hand does. A player holding only one pair wouldn't have the confidence to raise on that street.
hand.explanation:
Answer: 4 5

Your opponent's hand makes sense with the betting pattern, as they had high card on the flop, then improved to one pair on the turn, and finally to two pair on the river, leading to their aggressive betting on the turn and river to build the pot.
